Intro to Brain Network Modelling {#ch-intro background-color="#6880af"} ## Intro to Brain Network Modelling {#ch1-build-slide background-iframe="brain-build.html" background-color="#0a1622"} ​​​​​​ ::: {.notes} The opener for the whole course — build it up live, one click at a time: first the **brain** (the cortical surface), then the **wiring** (white-matter tracts connecting regions), then the **dynamics** (activity firing and travelling along those tracts), and finally the **network** itself (each region as a node). That progression *is* brain network modelling: anatomy gives the scaffold, the connectome gives the edges, and local dynamics on each node give the behaviour. Everything in the rest of the course hangs on this picture. ::: ## Why network models? Same lesion, different outcomes :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="55%"} ::: {.incremental} - Two patients, similar lesion location and volume - Different deficits, different recovery trajectories - The locus alone doesn't predict outcome - Function lives in the **network**: regional damage propagates through the connections that survive ::: ::: ::: {.column width="45%"} ![Same lesion, same network position, but a hub lesion (left, degree 6) wipes out most of the network's input, while a peripheral lesion (right, degree 1) barely touches it.](img/section-1/lesion_network.png){fig-align="center"} ::: :::: ::: {.fragment} **To predict outcome, model the whole network, not a catalogue of regions.** ::: ## Why dynamics? The brain is never idle :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="55%"} ::: {.incremental} - At rest, the brain shows **structured spontaneous activity**, not noise - Resting-state networks are reproducible across subjects and species - Tasks are perturbations of this ongoing dynamical state - Like a tennis player at the baseline: never still, poised for the next service ::: ::: {.fragment} **A model has to reproduce the resting repertoire, not only stimulus responses.** ::: ::: ::: {.column width="45%"} ![Resting activity for 8 regions in 3 modules. Top: traces wiggle in sync within each module, decoupled across them. Bottom: the resulting FC matrix shows three clear blocks.](img/section-1/rest_structured.png){fig-align="center" width="50%"} ::: :::: ## Outputs match what we measure :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.incremental} - TVB outputs **what scanners record:** EEG · MEG · SEEG · fMRI BOLD - Each modality is a **forward model** ($h$) on the same simulated state - Timescales come for free, EEG/SEEG in **ms**, BOLD in **seconds** ::: ::: {.fragment} **Direct comparison with data, no translation gap.** ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![One simulated state, three observables. Top: EEG/MEG-like fast oscillations. Middle: SEEG with sharp transients. Bottom: BOLD, the slow envelope through the HRF.](img/section-1/observables_multimodal.png){fig-align="center"} ::: :::: ## A short history of TVB ![](img/section-1/tvb_history_timeline.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"} ## From scans to a simulator: the TVB recipe ![](img/fig-tvb-recipe.png){fig-align="center"} ::: {.smaller .fragment} | Ingredient | Symbol | Source | |---|---|---| | **Connectome** (weights, delays) | $A_{ij},\ \tau_{ij}$ | DTI / tractography | | **Local dynamics** | $f$ | Neural mass / mean-field model | | **Coupling** | $g$ | Network propagation, with delays | | **Observation** | $h$ | BOLD / EEG / MEG forward model | ::: ::: {.fragment .smaller} The next slides unpack each building block. ::: ## Building Block 1: The Connectome :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="25%"} ![](img/figures/basic_idea_imaging_workflow1.png) ::: ::: {.column width="25%" .fragment} ![](img/figures/basic_idea_imaging_workflow2.png) ::: ::: {.column width="25%" .fragment} ![](img/figures/basic_idea_imaging_workflow3.png) ::: ::: {.column width="25%" .fragment} ![](img/figures/basic_idea_imaging_workflow4.png) ::: :::: ::: {.fragment .smaller} **Diffusion MRI → tractography → parcellation → connectivity matrix.** The output is the structural backbone $A_{ij}$ (weights) and $\tau_{ij}$ (delays). ::: ## Building Block 2: Dynamical Systems :::: {.columns} ::: {.column style="width:30%; font-size: 0.62em;"} A mass $m$ on a spring with stiffness $k$.
Perpetual oscillation at $\omega = \sqrt{k/m}$. **State Equations:** $$ \dot{x} = v $$ $$ \dot{v} = - \frac{k}{m}*x $$ **State Variables** | **Variable** | **Initial Value** | **Unit** | **Description** | |--------------|-------------------|----------|-----------------| | $x$ | 2.0 | $\mathrm{m}$ | Displacement from equilibrium | | $v$ | 0.0 | $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{s}}$ | Velocity | **Parameters** | **Parameter** | **Value** | **Unit** | **Description** | |---------------|-----------|----------|-----------------| | $k$ | 0.0001 | $\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{m}}$ | Spring stiffness constant | | $m$ | 1.0 | $\mathrm{kg}$ | Mass of the oscillating body | ::: ::: {.column style="width:70%;"} ::: {.spring-stack} ![](img/figures/dynamical_systems/fig-dynamical_systems_springs_single_thumb.png){.fragment .spring-frame} ![](img/figures/dynamical_systems/fig-dynamical_systems_springs_single.gif){.fragment .spring-frame} ::: ::: :::: ## Initial Conditions {background-color="#ffffff"} :::: {.columns} ::: {.column style="width:30%; font-size: 0.62em;"} Same spring-mass system as before. **What changes?** - Initial displacement only: $x_0 \in \{-1,-2,-3\}$ - Velocity stays fixed at $v_0 = 0$ - Parameters stay fixed: $k=10^{-4},\; m=1$ **What do we see?** - Same frequency in all cases - Larger initial stretch gives larger amplitude - Closed motion stays perfectly periodic ::: ::: {.column style="width:70%;"} ::: {.spring-stack} ![](img/figures/dynamical_systems/fig-dynamical_systems_springs_ics_thumb.png){.fragment .spring-frame} ![](img/figures/dynamical_systems/fig-dynamical_systems_springs_ics.gif){.fragment .spring-frame} ::: ::: :::: ## Phase Space {background-color="#ffffff"} :::: {.columns} ::: {.column style="width:30%; font-size: 0.62em;"} Same spring-mass system. Only the representation changes. **What changes?** - Plot state as $(x,v)$ instead of $x(t)$ - Use several initial conditions - Add the vector field of the dynamics **What do we see?** - Closed trajectories around equilibrium - Conservative dynamics with no damping - Geometry of the flow instead of time evolution ::: ::: {.column style="width:70%;"} ![](img/figures/dynamical_systems/fig-dynamical_systems_springs_phase_plane.gif){fig-align="center"} ::: :::: ## Parameters — Mass {background-color="#ffffff"} :::: {.columns} ::: {.column style="width:30%; font-size: 0.62em;"} Same equations and same initial condition. Only the mass changes. **What changes?** - $m \in \{0.5, 1.0, 2.0\}$ - $x_0=-2$, $v_0=0$ stay fixed - $k$ stays fixed **What do we see?** - Frequency follows $\omega = \sqrt{k/m}$ - Heavier masses oscillate more slowly - Box size encodes the mass value visually ::: ::: {.column style="width:70%;"} ::: {.spring-stack} ![](img/figures/dynamical_systems/fig-dynamical_systems_springs_mass_thumb.png){.fragment .spring-frame} ![](img/figures/dynamical_systems/fig-dynamical_systems_springs_mass.gif){.fragment .spring-frame} ::: ::: :::: ## Towards Realism — Damping & Load {background-color="#ffffff"} :::: {.columns} ::: {.column style="width:30%; font-size: 0.62em;"} Now we modify the toy model toward a more realistic oscillator. **What changes?** $$ \dot{v} = -\frac{k}{m}x - \frac{c}{m}v + g_{\mathrm{eff}} $$ - Add damping: $c=0.006$ - Add constant load: $g_{\mathrm{eff}}=0.00015$ **What do we see?** - Oscillations decay over time - Equilibrium shifts away from zero - Compare ideal toy model vs damped + load ::: ::: {.column style="width:70%;"} ::: {.spring-stack} ![](img/figures/dynamical_systems/fig-dynamical_systems_springs_realism_thumb.png){.fragment .spring-frame} ![](img/figures/dynamical_systems/fig-dynamical_systems_springs_realism.gif){.fragment .spring-frame} ::: ::: :::: ## Phase Space: Damped Spring :::: {.columns} ::: {.column} **Spring Oscillator** ![](img/figures/dynamical_systems/fig-dynamical_systems_springs_phase_plane.gif) ::: ::: {.column} **Damped Spring** ![](img/figures/dynamical_systems/fig-dynamical_systems_springs_realism_phase_plane.gif) ::: :::: # Towards Brain Dynamics: Mean Field Models {background-video="img/videos/birds.mp4" background-video-loop="true" background-video-muted="true"} ## Mean field models & Neural Masses ![](img/mean_field_overview.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"} - Population rate: $r(t) = \langle \#\text{spikes}\rangle / (N\,\Delta t)$ - Jansen-Rit (3 populations): PC = $y_1 - y_2$, the EEG-like PSP at pyramidal cells ## EEG-Like Activity with the Jansen-Rit-Model :::: {.columns} ::: {.column style="width:50%; font-size: 0.45em; height: 660px; overflow-y: scroll;"} **@Jansen1995** Lumped-parameter cortical column model with three neural populations: pyramidal cells, excitatory interneurons, and inhibitory interneurons. Each population is modeled by a PSP block (2nd-order linear filter) and a sigmoidal potential-to-firing-rate transformation. **Derived Variables** $$ v_{pyr} = y_{1} - y_{2} $$ **State Equations** $$ \dot{y_{0}} = y_{3} $$ $$ \dot{y_{3}} = - y_{0}*a^{2} - 2*a*y_{3} + A*a*\operatorname{Sigm}{\left(y_{1} - y_{2} \right)} $$ $$ \dot{y_{1}} = y_{4} $$ $$ \dot{y_{4}} = - y_{1}*a^{2} - 2*a*y_{4} + A*a*\left(p + C_{2}*\operatorname{Sigm}{\left(C_{1}*y_{0} \right)} + K*c_{intercolumn}\right) $$ $$ \dot{y_{2}} = y_{5} $$ $$ \dot{y_{5}} = - y_{2}*b^{2} - 2*b*y_{5} + B*C_{4}*b*\operatorname{Sigm}{\left(C_{3}*y_{0} \right)} $$ with $$ \operatorname{Sigm}{\left(v \right)} = \frac{2*e_{0}}{1 + e^{r*\left(v_{0} - v\right)}} $$ *State Variables* | **Variable** | **Initial Value** | **Unit** | **Description** | |--------------|-------------------|----------|-----------------| | $y_{0}$ | 0.1 | $\mathrm{mV}$ | | | $y_{3}$ | 0.1 | $\frac{\mathrm{mV}}{\mathrm{s}}$ | | | $y_{1}$ | 0.1 | $\mathrm{mV}$ | | | $y_{4}$ | 0.1 | $\frac{\mathrm{mV}}{\mathrm{s}}$ | | | $y_{2}$ | 0.1 | $\mathrm{mV}$ | | | $y_{5}$ | 0.1 | $\frac{\mathrm{mV}}{\mathrm{s}}$ | | *Parameters* | **Parameter** | **Value** | **Unit** | **Description** | |---------------|-----------|----------|-----------------| | $A$ | 3.25 | $\mathrm{mV}$ | Maximum amplitude of excitatory PSP (EPSP) | | $B$ | 22 | $\mathrm{mV}$ | Maximum amplitude of inhibitory PSP (IPSP) | | $C$ | 135 | — | Lumped connectivity constant (C = C1) | | $a$ | 100 | $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ | Reciprocal of EPSP time constant | | $b$ | 50 | $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ | Reciprocal of IPSP time constant | | $v_{0}$ | 6 | $\mathrm{mV}$ | PSP for 50% firing rate (sigmoid midpoint) | | $e_{0}$ | 2.5 | $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ | Half of maximum firing rate | | $r$ | 0.56 | $\mathrm{mV}^{-1}$ | Steepness of sigmoid transformation | | $p$ | 220 | $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ | Mean external input (afferent pulse density) | | $K$ | 0 | — | Per-node coupling strength scaling (K[i] scales coupling received by node i) | **Derived Parameters** $$ C_{1} = C $$ $$ C_{2} = 0.8*C $$ $$ C_{3} = 0.25*C $$ $$ C_{4} = 0.25*C $$ ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](img/figures/jansen1995/fig-jr-singlenode.png) ::: :::: ## Building Block 3: Coupling → Network Dynamics ::: {#321b7cfd .cell execution_count=1} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ![](slides_files/figure-revealjs/cell-2-output-1.png){} ::: ::: ## Uncoupled system
**@Kuramoto1975** Phase oscillator model. Each node evolves with its own natural frequency $\omega$; coupling $I$ is added by the network. **State Equations** $$\dot{\theta} = \omega$$ *State Variables* | Variable | Initial value | Unit | |---|---|---| | $\theta$ | $\sim\mathcal{U}(0,2\pi)$ | — | **Derived Variables** $$activity = \sin(\theta)$$ *Parameters* | Parameter | Value | Unit | Description | |---|---|---|---| | $\omega$ | 1.0 | — | Natural frequency | *Simulation settings* | | | |---|---| | Duration | 100 s | | Step size | 0.01 s | | Integrator | Heun | | Nodes | 3 (V1, V2, MT) | | Coupling $K$ | 0.0 (uncoupled) |
![](img/videos/NetworkDynamics/uncoupled_kuramoto.gif){width="100%"}
## Coupled system {.nostretch}
**@Kuramoto1975** — same model as before, now with coupling enabled. **State Equations** $$\dot{\theta} = I + \omega$$ **Coupling: $I$** $$ I = \frac{K \sum_{j=0}^{-1 + N} - {g}_{i,j} \sin{\left(\theta_{i} - \theta_{j} \right)}}{3} $$ Receives $\theta$ from connected regions. **Pre-synaptic:** $c_{\text{pre}} = \sin(\theta_j - \theta_i)$ **Post-synaptic:** $c_{\text{post}} = \dfrac{K \cdot gx}{3}$ *Coupling parameters* | Parameter | Value | Description | |---|---|---| | $K$ | 0.4 | Global coupling strength | *Network* | Edge | Weight | |---|---| | V1 → V2 | 0.7 | | V2 → MT | 0.4 | | V1 → MT | 0.2 | *Simulation settings* | | | |---|---| | Duration | 100 s | | Step size | 0.01 s | | Integrator | Heun |
![](img/videos/NetworkDynamics/coupled_kuramoto.gif){width="100%"}
## Mathematical framework
$$dS_i = \left[f_d(S_i, \theta^d, C_i, I_i) \right]dt + g(S_i, \theta^g)\, dW_i$$
**State Evolution** - $S_i$ - state variables at node $i$ - $f_d$ - dynamics function with parameters $\theta^d$ - $C_i$ - coupling input from connected nodes - $I_i$ - external input (stimulation, driving signals) - $g$ - noise diffusion coefficient with parameters $\theta^g$ - $dW_i$ - Wiener process (stochastic fluctuations)
::: {#64af177e .cell execution_count=2} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ![](slides_files/figure-revealjs/cell-3-output-1.png){fig-align='center'} ::: :::
## Coupled Brain Network :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width=20%} ![](img/fig-network_visualization_rows.png){height=590px} ::: ::: {.column width=80%} ::: {.fragment fragment-index=2} $$ C_i = f_c^{\text{post}}\Big(\sum_j A_{ij}\, f_c^{\text{pre}}(S_i, S_j(t-\tau_{ij}), \theta^c), S_i, \theta^c\Big) $$ ::: {style="font-size: 0.55em;"} **Coupling** - $f_c^{\text{pre}}$ - pre-aggregation transformation - $A_{ij}$ - structural connectivity weight ($j \to i$) - $\tau_{ij}$ - transmission delay (tract length / conduction speed) - $f_c^{\text{post}}$ - post-aggregation transformation - $\theta^c$ - coupling parameters ::: ::: ::: {.fragment fragment-index=3} ![](img/coupling_schematic.png){height="250px" fig-align="center"} ::: ::: :::: # 2. Defining a FAIR Brain Network Model {background-color="#6880af"} ## FAIR Guiding Principles ![[@TuringCommunity2024]](img/fair/reproducible-definitions.svg){fig-align="center" height="600px"} ## FAIR Principles ![[@TuringCommunity2024]](img/fair/fair-principles.svg){fig-align="center" height="600px"} ## A Simulation Experiment in Computational Neuroscience ![](img/tvbo/tvbo-network-insets.png) ## A Simulation Experiment in Computational Neuroscience ```{=html}

``` ## The Virtual Brain Ontology (TVB-O) {#tvbo-intro} A brain network model usually lives in a methods section — prose you can *read* but rarely **re-run**. TVB-O makes the **model itself a FAIR, machine-readable artifact**: ::: {.incremental} - **Common vocabulary** — ontology-backed terms with persistent identifiers (`w3id.org/tvbo`); no more ambiguous symbols across papers - **Machine-readable metadata** — a compact YAML schema captures everything needed to reproduce a simulation - **Curated database** — published models, connectomes and study configurations, ready to load and run - **Code generation** — one specification → executable code for TVB, JAX, Julia, … ::: ![[@Martin2025]](img/figures/tvbo/tvbo_logo.png){fig-align="center" height=120px} ::: {.notes} The single slide that introduces the ontology the whole course builds on. The problem: a model published in a paper is prose plus symbols — human-readable but not machine-actionable, so reproducing it is slow and error-prone. TVB-O makes the specification *itself* the shareable object, and four things follow: a common ontology-backed vocabulary (terms resolve to persistent identifiers), a compact machine-readable YAML schema, a curated database of ready-to-run models and connectomes, and code generation to multiple backends (TVB, JAX, Julia). The next slides show what that specification looks like in practice. ::: ## Model Specification with TVB-O :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width=35%} **Metadata Schema** ```yaml id: 21 model: Dynamics dynamics: name: Dynamics parameters: a: name: a value: 1.0 state_variables: x: name: x equation: rhs: a*x initial_value: 1.0 integration: method: Heun time_scale: ms duration: 4.0 step_size: 0.01 explorations: name: a_sweep space: - parameter: a explored_values: - 1.0 - 0.0 - -1.0 ``` ::: ::: {.column width=65%} **Python API** ```python from tvbo import DynamicalSystem, SimulationExperiment from tvbo.datamodel.schema import Exploration, ExplorationAxis bsplot.style.use("tvbo") g = DynamicalSystem( state_variables={"x": { "equation": {"rhs": "a*x"}, "initial_value": 1.0}}, parameters={"a": {"value": 1.0}}, ) exp = SimulationExperiment(dynamics=g) exp.explorations = Exploration( name="a_sweep", space=ExplorationAxis( parameter="a", explored_values=[1.0, 0, -1.0] ), ) exp.integration.duration = 4.0 exp.integration.step_size = 0.01 res = exp.run("tvboptim") ``` ::: :::: ## Specifying an experiment with TVB-O ![](img/figures/tvbo/fig-overview.png){height=680px fig-align="center"} ## Build an experiment in the browser No code — assemble a model, connectome and stimulus, then run it. The platform writes the reproducible specification for you. ![](img/videos/tvbo-experiment-builder-workflow.mp4){width="820px" height="540px" fig-align="center" autoplay="" loop="" muted="" controls=""} ::: {.notes} This is the TVB-O experiment builder in the browser: choose a neural-mass model, attach a connectome, add a stimulus, and launch — point and click. Under the hood it produces exactly the machine-readable specification we just saw, so even a click-through experiment stays fully reproducible and shareable. ::: ## ...or run it in Python The very same specification, driven from a notebook — for scripting, parameter sweeps and pipelines. ![](img/videos/tvbo-run-in-python.mp4){width="820px" height="540px" fig-align="center" autoplay="" loop="" muted="" controls=""} ::: {.notes} The builder and the Python API are two front-ends to one specification. Prototype visually, then move to code for parameter sweeps, HPC runs or embedding in a pipeline — without re-specifying anything. This is the bridge from the no-code demo to the hands-on part of the course. ::: ## ...or explore the ontology and simulate in JupyterLab `tvb-ext-ontology` turns the model ontology into an interactive graph: search for a neural-mass model, inspect its definition and ontology relationships, add it to a workspace, pick a connectome — then **Run Simulation**, without ever leaving JupyterLab. ![](img/videos/tvbo-ext-ontology-simulation.mp4){width="720px" height="548px" fig-align="center" autoplay="" loop="" muted="" controls=""} ::: {.notes} A third front-end onto the same specification — this time inside JupyterLab. The `tvb-ext-ontology` extension renders the TVB-O ontology as a force-directed graph you can search and explore. Click a neural-mass model to read its definition and see its parents and children in the ontology, then add it to the workspace. Choose a parcellation and tractogram for the connectome and press Run Simulation: the server builds the experiment straight from the ontology and runs it, writing BIDS-formatted results that appear right in the file browser, ready to open in a notebook. Browse, assemble, simulate — all from the ontology graph itself. ::: ## Try TVB-O yourself {.smaller} Three front-ends, one specification — scan to open. :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="33%"} **Platform** · [tvbo.charite.de](https://tvbo.charite.de/) ![](img/qr/preview-tvbo-platform.png){height="240px" fig-align="center" style="border:1px solid #ddd"} ![](img/qr/qr-tvbo-platform.png){height="150px" fig-align="center"} ::: ::: {.column width="33%"} **Python package** · `pip install tvbo` ![](img/qr/preview-tvbo-pypi.png){height="240px" fig-align="center" style="border:1px solid #ddd"} ![](img/qr/qr-tvbo-pypi.png){height="150px" fig-align="center"} ::: ::: {.column width="33%"} **JupyterLab extension** · `pip install tvb-ext-ontology` ![](img/qr/preview-tvb-ext-ontology-pypi.png){height="240px" fig-align="center" style="border:1px solid #ddd"} ![](img/qr/qr-tvb-ext-ontology-pypi.png){height="150px" fig-align="center"} ::: :::: ::: {.notes} Everything shown is public and installable today. The platform at tvbo.charite.de runs the browser builder and the ontology knowledge graph; `pip install tvbo` gives you the Python package and the machine-readable specification; `pip install tvb-ext-ontology` adds the JupyterLab extension you just saw. Same specification underneath all three — scan whichever way of working suits you. ::: ## In short {#recap-framework background-iframe="brain-recap.html" background-color="#0a1622"} ::: {.recap-box} A brain network model is **local dynamics on a structural connectome**, observed through a **forward model**. ::: {.incremental} - **Regions** carry neural-mass **dynamics** — the local node model - Wired by the **connectome** — weights and conduction **delays** - A **forward model** maps activity to **BOLD, EEG, MEG** ::: ::: {.recap-tvbo} **TVB-O** isn't a piece of software — it's a whole **framework** that harmonizes *how* such models are specified, so any of them stays shareable and re-runnable. ::: ::: ::: {.notes} Recap of the framework before we turn to applications: a brain network model couples local regional dynamics over the structural connectome — with connection weights and conduction delays — and a forward model turns the simulated activity into measurable signals like BOLD, EEG or MEG. That whole construct is what's rotating here. TVB-O is not a tool you run; it's a framework for harmonizing how those models are specified, so any of them can be shared and re-run. ::: ## Applications in basic research :::: {.columns} ::: {.column} ![](img/figures/applications/Schirner2023Title.png) ![[@Schirner2023]](img/videos/Schirner2023videosnippet.mp4){width="100%" autoplay="" loop="" muted=""} ::: ::: {.column} ![](img/figures/applications/Koller2024Title.png) ![[@Koller2024]](img/videos/travelling_waves.mp4){width="100%" autoplay="" loop="" muted=""} ::: :::: # 4. Clinical Applications {background-color="#6880af"} ## From mechanism to the clinic Brain disorders are disorders of brain **dynamics**. A *personalized virtual brain* turns a patient's scans into a working model — to expose a mechanism we cannot measure, and to rehearse a treatment before it reaches the patient. ```{dot} //| fig-align: center //| fig-height: 3.0 digraph recipe { rankdir=LR; bgcolor="transparent"; nodesep=0.30; ranksep=0.80; node [shape=box, style="filled,rounded", fontname="Helvetica", fontsize=13, penwidth=1.4, margin="0.20,0.15", color="#5ea8d4", fontcolor="#1c2b3a"]; edge [color="#6880af", penwidth=1.7, arrowsize=0.85, fontname="Helvetica", fontsize=11, fontcolor="#6880af"]; data [label="Patient data\nMRI · dMRI · EEG / MEG", fillcolor="#e2f2fb"]; model [label="Personalized\nvirtual brain", fillcolor="#bee0f4"]; mech [label="Hidden mechanism\nexcitability · E/I · connectivity", fillcolor="#cfe8d6"]; clinic [label="Clinical use\ndiagnose · predict · plan therapy", fillcolor="#f0e0c4"]; data -> model [label=" build "]; model -> mech [label=" simulate & fit "]; mech -> clinic [label=" infer "]; clinic -> data [label=" personalize ", constraint=false, style=dashed, color="#9ab5b8", fontcolor="#9ab5b8"]; } ``` ::: {.notes} **One idea for the whole chapter:** brain disorders are disorders of *dynamics* — so rebuild each patient's brain as a network model and let it run. - **Build** — patient scans (MRI · dMRI · EEG/MEG) → a personalized virtual brain - **Simulate & fit** — match the model to that patient's data - **Infer** — read off a mechanism you *can't* measure directly: excitability · E/I balance · effective connectivity - **Use** — predict an outcome, or rehearse a stimulation/therapy *in silico* before touching the patient The dashed arrow = the personalized-medicine loop: the clinical question shapes the next measurement. Next slides: where this recipe has actually reached patients, disease by disease. ::: ## The clinical landscape — ICD-11 areas → node dynamical models ::: {#062991ef .cell execution_count=3} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](slides_files/figure-revealjs/cell-4-output-1.svg){fig-align='center'} ::: ::: ::: {.notes} **The map:** where whole-brain *dynamical* modeling has reached patients — ICD-11 chapter → clinical domain → the node model each area uses. - Counts = distinct, PubMed-verified applications; model leaves read from each study's full text - **Most areas = Bifurcation Analysis {#ch-bifurcation background-color="#6880af"} ## How to analyse a brain system ![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-bifurcation-overview.png){fig-align="center" width="96%"} ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="33%" .smaller} **Time series** $x(t)$ at fixed $(a, x_0)$ ::: ::: {.column width="33%" .smaller} **Phase plane** flow geometry at fixed $a$ ::: ::: {.column width="34%" .smaller} **Bifurcation diagram** regimes as $a$ varies ::: ::: ::: {.notes} Three complementary lenses on the same system. The **time series** is what you'd actually record — but it only shows one parameter setting. The **phase plane** shows the *geometry* of the flow at a fixed parameter: where trajectories go, what's stable. The **bifurcation diagram** is the summary — it tracks how the equilibria and their stability change as we turn one knob ($a$). Goal for this chapter: learn to read that third panel. It's a *map of regimes*, and it tells you what behaviours a model can produce before you run a single long simulation. ::: ## Linear Systems :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%" .r-fit-text} $$ \dot x = a\,x \quad\Rightarrow\quad x(t) = x_0\,e^{a t} $$ - $x(t)$: State variable - $x_0$: Initial condition - $a$: Parameter Solution: $$ x(t) = x_0\,e^{a t} $$ Equilibrium: $$ \dot{x}(t) = ax(t) = 0? $$ $$ \quad\Rightarrow\quad x_{eq} = 0 $$ ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.r-stack } ![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-stability.png) ![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-stability-perturbation.png){.fragment} ::: ::: :::: ::: {.notes} Start with the simplest possible system — 1D and linear. Everything about stability is in the sign of $a$. The solution is an exponential: if $a<0$ any perturbation decays back to zero, so the equilibrium is stable; if $a>0$ it grows, so it's unstable; $a=0$ is the knife-edge. The equilibrium is always $x=0$, but its *stability* flips as $a$ passes through zero. The second figure makes this concrete: nudge the system off the equilibrium and, when it's stable, it returns. Neural masses work the same way — we linearise around a fixed point, and the sign of the eigenvalues tells us whether activity settles back or runs away. ::: ## 2D portraits ::: {.r-stack} ![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-bifurcation-phase-portraits-animation.gif){.fragment} ![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-bifurcation-phase-portraits-static.png){.fragment} ::: ::: {.notes} Two ways to read the same phase portraits. First the animation: watch the flow reorganise as the parameter sweeps — fixed points appear, move, change stability, and a closed loop (a limit cycle) emerges. Then the static snapshots: the same regimes frozen side by side, left-to-right from a single stable resting state, through the bifurcation, to a sustained oscillation. Takeaway: small smooth parameter changes cause *qualitative* changes in behaviour — that's a bifurcation. ::: ## Regime-change building blocks :::: {.columns} ::: {.column .smaller width=20%} - Top: equilibria as $a$ varies. - Bottom: local flow $\dot x=f(x)$ at the dotted parameter slice. ::: ::: {.column width=80%} ![](img/normal_forms.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"} ::: :::: ::: {.notes} There's only a small zoo of *local* bifurcations — the normal forms. Top panel: equilibria and their stability (solid = stable, dashed = unstable) as the parameter varies. Bottom: the 1D flow at the dotted slice. The classics are saddle-node (two fixed points collide and vanish), transcritical (they swap stability), pitchfork (one splits into three), and Hopf (next slide). You don't have to memorise them — just know that almost every regime change in a neural model is one of these in disguise. ::: ## Hopf: birth of an oscillation $$ \dot z = (a + i\omega)z - |z|^2z, \qquad r_{\mathrm{cycle}} = \sqrt a $$ ![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-bifurcation-hopf-birth.gif){fig-align="center" width="100%"} ::: {.smaller} fixed point loses stability $\Rightarrow$ stable oscillation appears ::: ::: {.notes} This is the important one for brain dynamics, because it's how rhythms are born. As $a$ crosses zero, a stable fixed point loses stability and a stable limit cycle appears around it, with radius growing like $\sqrt a$ — so the oscillation starts small and grows. Mechanistically: the system stops settling to a constant rate and starts oscillating (think alpha or gamma rhythms emerging). One-sentence version: **a Hopf bifurcation is where a quiet equilibrium turns into a spontaneous oscillation.** ::: ## Dynamical Regimes ![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-bifurcation_timeseries.png) ::: {.notes} Tie it back to the time series — the thing experimentalists actually see. Each parameter regime produces a characteristically different trace: a flat/noisy fixed point, a damped transient, or a sustained oscillation. The bifurcation diagram predicts *which* of these you'll get, so instead of guessing parameters by trial and error you read them off the map. ::: ## Numerical continuation {.r-fit-text} ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="62%"} ![](img/continuation_pseudo_arclength_white.png){height=600px} ::: ::: {.column width="38%" .smaller} $$ F(x, a)=0, \qquad (x,a)=(x(s),a(s)) $$ - follow branches through folds - detect folds and Hopf points - switch to periodic orbits ::: ::: ::: {.notes} How do we compute these diagrams without brute-force simulating millions of settings? **Continuation.** We solve $F(x,a)=0$ and *follow* the solution branch as $a$ changes — like walking along a ridge. Pseudo-arclength parametrises by arc length $s$ so we can turn around "folds" where the curve bends back on itself (a plain parameter sweep would just fail there). Along the way we automatically detect folds and Hopf points and can switch onto the periodic-orbit branch. This is what tools like BifurcationKit.jl do. ::: ## TVB simulation infused with bifurcation analysis ![](img/g2d_network_bifurcation.gif){fig-align="center" width="100%"} ::: {.smaller} TVBO + tvboptim simulation of Generic2dOscillator on the Desikan-Killiany network. Nodes are colored by their instantaneous $V(t)$ and projected onto axial (top) and sagittal views; the regime map places each node at its current $(I_{\mathrm{eff}}, V)$ on the bifurcation diagram. ::: ::: {.notes} Now scale it up to a whole brain: a Generic-2D-Oscillator on the Desikan-Killiany connectome. Each node is coloured by its instantaneous state and placed on the shared bifurcation diagram — so you can literally watch which regime each region is in, and how coupling pushes nodes across bifurcations. The point: bifurcation analysis isn't just a single-node toy, it organises the dynamics of the full network model. ::: ## Bifurcation analysis: hands-on - Notebook: [`notebooks/4_BifurcationAnalysis/BifurcationAnalysis.qmd`](notebooks/4_BifurcationAnalysis/BifurcationAnalysis.qmd) - Sweep input $I$ in a TVB neural mass model - Continue fixed points and periodic orbits - Read the diagram as a **map of regimes** ```python result = exp.run("bifurcationkit.jl") ``` ::: {.notes} What you'll do in the notebook: take a TVB neural-mass model, sweep the input current $I$, and continue both the fixed points and the periodic orbits. The mindset to keep: read the resulting diagram as a *map of regimes* — for any parameter value it tells you what the model will do. The continuation itself runs through the Julia backend in one line. ::: # 7. Stimulating the Brain {background-color="#6880af"} ## Why stimulate a brain model? :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="55%"} ::: {.incremental} - At rest the brain is a **dynamical system poised near a regime boundary** ([Intro](#ch-intro), [Bifurcation](#ch-bifurcation)) - Tasks, **sensory flashes**, **TMS**, and **DBS** are all *perturbations* of that ongoing state - A stimulus lets us **probe the regime** — does a nudge decay, or tip the system into a new behaviour? - ... **reproduce evoked responses** we record (EEG/MEG/SEEG) - ... and **test interventions** *in silico* before touching a patient ::: ::: ::: {.column width="45%"} ::: {.fragment} ![A small perturbation off a stable equilibrium: does it relax back, or grow? Stimulation asks exactly this question — now driven by an external signal.](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-stability-perturbation.png){fig-align="center" width="92%"} ::: ::: :::: ::: {.notes} Tie this straight back to chapters 1 and 5. In chapter 1 we said the brain is never idle — resting activity is structured, and tasks are perturbations of that ongoing dynamical state. In chapter 5 we learned to read a model as a *map of regimes*. Stimulation is where those two ideas meet: we inject an external signal and watch how the system responds. The same machinery covers a visual flash, a TMS pulse, or a DBS train — they differ only in *where*, *when*, and *how strong*. The figure is the chapter-5 "nudge the equilibrium" picture; stimulation just makes that nudge an explicit, time-dependent input we control. ::: ## Stimulation is a time-dependent `Event` In TVB-O a stimulus is an **`Event`**: a *symbolic, time-dependent perturbation* injected into the dynamics. ::: {.fragment} | field | meaning | |---|---| | `name` | identifier — **and the model port it drives** | | `event_type` | `"stimulus"` for external input | | `parameters` | named knobs (`onset`, `width`, `amplitude`, …) | | `equation.rhs` | any expression in `t` **and** the parameters | | `nodes` / `weights` | *where* it lands — which nodes, how strongly | ::: ::: {.fragment} Because the signal is **symbolic**, the same object serializes to YAML, renders to every backend, and is fully **FAIR** — just like the model itself. ::: ::: {.notes} The key idea: a stimulus is not a hardcoded array of numbers, it's a symbolic equation in time. That's what makes it portable and reproducible — it round-trips through the ontology like every other part of the experiment. The fields map onto the three questions any stimulus has to answer: what shape (equation + parameters), where (nodes/weights), and into what (the model port its name binds to). Don't dwell on the table — we'll see the main fields appear in the next slides. ::: ## The recipe: **define → inject → run** ::: {.incremental} 1. **Define** the signal as an `Event` — a shape in time 2. **Inject** it: register the event and add its variable to a state equation's right-hand side 3. **Run** and compare the resting vs. stimulated time series ::: ::: {.fragment} We start with a **single Generic-2d-Oscillator** node — the same model whose bifurcation diagram we read in [Bifurcation Analysis](#ch-bifurcation) — then place it in a small network. ::: ## Step 0 — A baseline run :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="48%" .smaller} ```{.python} from tvbo import Dynamics, SimulationExperiment from tvbo.datamodel.schema import Event g2d = Dynamics.from_db("Generic2dOscillator") exp = SimulationExperiment(dynamics=g2d) res = exp.run() res.integration.sel(variable="V").plot() ``` With its database defaults ($I = 0$) the node relaxes onto a **stable focus** — far from any oscillation. This is our *resting state*. ::: ::: {.column width="52%"} ::: {#563d93e4 .cell execution_count=4} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](slides_files/figure-revealjs/cell-5-output-1.png){fig-align='center'} ::: ::: ::: :::: ::: {.notes} Before stimulating, establish the baseline. Loaded straight from the database, the Generic-2d oscillator sits on a stable fixed point: whatever the initial transient, it settles to a constant — the "quiet equilibrium" from the Hopf slide in chapter 5. Everything interesting on the next slides is the *departure* from this flat line. ::: ## Step 1 — Define the stimulus :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="48%" .smaller} A rectangular pulse, written as a `Piecewise` in `t`: ```{.python} event = Event( name="stim", event_type="stimulus", parameters={ "onset": {"value": 200.0}, "width": {"value": 200.0}, "amplitude": {"value": 5.0}, }, equation={"rhs": "Piecewise((amplitude," " (t >= onset) & (t < onset + width))," " (0.0, True))"}, ) event.plot() ``` Any expression in `t` and the event's parameters is valid — a Gaussian, a ramp, or a sinusoid works the same way. ::: ::: {.column width="52%"} ::: {#d27e15de .cell execution_count=5} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](slides_files/figure-revealjs/cell-6-output-1.png){fig-align='center'} ::: ::: ::: :::: ::: {.notes} The equation is the whole stimulus: a box at `amplitude` while onset ≤ t < onset+width, zero otherwise. The parameters are named knobs, so onset/width/amplitude become things you can sweep or even *infer* later (the bridge to chapter 6). Because it's symbolic, a Gaussian bump or an oscillating drive is a one-line change to the rhs — nothing else in the recipe moves. ::: ## Step 2 — Expose a port, bind the event The model author places a **stimulation port** `stim` (default `0`) where input physically enters; the event binds to it **by name**. ```{.python} from tvbo.datamodel.schema import Parameter # the model exposes a stimulation port `stim`, defaulting to 0 g2d.parameters["stim"] = Parameter(name="stim", value=0.0) g2d.state_variables["V"].equation.rhs = ( "d*tau*((I + stim)*gamma - V**3*f + V**2*e + V*g " "+ V*local_coupling + W*alpha + c_glob*gamma)" ) exp.events["stim"] = event # event.name == port symbol → bound ``` The port is authored **once**, at the right spot in the equation. With no event `stim = 0` → the resting model; attaching the event drives it. The equations are never rewritten per experiment, and nothing is injected implicitly. *(Jansen-Rit ships exactly such a port — `P` — see the use-case.)* ::: {.notes} This is the conceptual heart of the chapter, and the clean design. Rather than mutating the model's equation per experiment, the model *exposes a port* — a named input symbol (`stim`) placed where external drive physically enters, defaulting to zero. The event binds to that port by name. Two payoffs: the model stays invariant (no event → stim=0 → rest, attach event → driven, no equation surgery), and placement is explicit, so there is no hidden additive assumption — the author decides where and how the input enters. This is exactly the `P` input port the Jansen-Rit model ships with. ::: ## Step 3 — Run: rest → stimulus → rest :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="54%"} ::: {#e0de22cc .cell execution_count=6} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](slides_files/figure-revealjs/cell-7-output-1.png){fig-align='center'} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="46%"} ![](img/figures/stimulation/fig-g2d-bifurcation-hopf.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"} ::: :::: ::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"} While the pulse is on, it lifts the **effective input** $I + \text{stim} = 5$ **above the node's Hopf bifurcation** ($\approx 3.5$), into the **limit-cycle envelope** (right). A limit cycle is born for the pulse's duration, then activity relaxes back to rest — the Hopf mechanism from [Bifurcation Analysis](#ch-bifurcation), triggered on demand. ::: ::: {.notes} Payoff slide. Left: the trace is flat, oscillates during the grey pulse window, then flattens again. Right: *why* — the bifurcation diagram of this exact G2D. Rest sits at I=0 on the stable branch (green); the pulse adds 5, landing at I=5 inside the limit-cycle envelope (red), past the Hopf at ~3.5. So stimulation and bifurcation analysis are the same story from two sides: the diagram *predicts* that a strong-enough pulse triggers oscillation, and the time series shows it happen. ::: ## From one node to a network :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="44%" .smaller} Localize the stimulus to **one node** with `nodes` / `weights`: ```{.python} event = Event( name="stim", event_type="stimulus", parameters={"onset": {"value": 80.0}, "duration": {"value": 5.0}, "amplitude": {"value": 5.0}}, equation={"rhs": "Piecewise((amplitude," " (t>=onset)&(t- A*a*Sigm(y1 - y2) - 2*a*y3 - a**2*y0 y1: {equation: {rhs: y4}} y4: equation: rhs: >- A*a*(p + P + C2*Sigm(C1*y0) + z0) - 2*a*y4 - a**2*y1 y2: {equation: {rhs: y5}} y5: equation: rhs: >- B*b*(C4*Sigm(C3*y0)) - 2*b*y5 - b**2*y2 z0: {equation: {rhs: z1}} z1: equation: rhs: >- A*a_d*K*c_intercolumn - 2*a_d*z1 - a_d**2*z0 derived_variables: v_pyr: {equation: {rhs: y1 - y2}} output: [v_pyr] network: number_of_regions: 2 number_of_nodes: 2 conduction_speed: {value: 0} nodes: - {id: 0} - id: 1 parameters: B: {value: 17.6} C: {value: 108} edges: - source: 0 target: 1 directed: true - source: 1 target: 0 directed: true coupling: *sigmoidal_coupling events: *flash_events integration: *integration_vep execution: random_seed: 42 explorations: VEP_delayed_fig11: mode: zip n_trials: 40 average: trials parameters: K: element_domains: - element: 0 explored_values: [300, 100] - element: 1 explored_values: [1000, 4000] ``` :::: :::: {.column width="30%"} **Reproduced** ![](img/figures/jansen1995/reproduced/Jansen1995_Fig11.png) :::: ::::: ::: {.notes} The real-world payoff, using exactly the recipe we just built. A sensory flash is a stimulus `Event` (here `*flash_events`) injected into a two-column Jansen-Rit model. The middle column is the *full FAIR specification* — model, network, events, and the trial exploration — that reproduces Jansen & Rit's 1995 Figure 11 (right) from the original paper (left). Same pattern, scaled: symbolic event, injected into named state equations, run across trials. ::: ## Use-case: real music reshapes whole-brain FC :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="40%" .smaller} A real recording (`.wav` → loudness envelope) is passed as a **data file** and injected at **primary auditory cortex** (DK transverse-temporal) of a Reduced-Wong-Wang + BOLD whole-brain model. ```{.python} event = Event( name="acoustic", # input port event_type="stimulus", dataLocation="music.npy", # real waveform sampling_rate=0.1, # per ms parameters={"onset": {"value": 15000}}, nodes=[32, 81], # L/R auditory ctx weights=[1.0, 1.0], ) exp.events["acoustic"] = event res = exp.run("tvboptim") # BOLD + FC ``` Pure native-tvbo spec → tvboptim codegen. ::: ::: {.column width="60%"} ![](img/figures/stimulation/fig-acoustic-stimulation.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"} ::: :::: ::: {.smaller} Auditory BOLD tracks the music; resting FC matches empirical (**r = 0.31**); the FC change is **content-specific** — music vs an energy-matched control. ::: ::: {.notes} The capstone: stimuli are not limited to closed-form equations — they can be *real recordings*. We take an actual `.wav`, reduce it to a loudness envelope, and pass it as a data-file `Event` (`dataLocation` + `sampling_rate` + `onset`). The tvboptim backend interpolates it at each step and injects it into the primary auditory cortex (transverse-temporal gyrus) of a Reduced-Wong-Wang whole-brain model on the Desikan-Killiany connectome, observed through a Balloon-Windkessel BOLD model. Walk the figure: (1) the auditory-cortex BOLD *tracks the music's loudness* (the model is listening); (2) the resting model FC matches the empirical FC shipped with the connectome — global coupling G line-searched to r≈0.31 (single global scalar; reverse-mode-AD gradients are for the regional case); (3) the connectivity change is *content-specific* — we contrast music against an energy-matched **constant** control, so the effect is the sound's temporal structure, not the drive level. Left-right primary auditory coupling rises from ~0.18 (control) to ~1.0 (music), spreading into superior-temporal / insular / supramarginal cortex; the full ΔFC is shown with L/R primary auditory marked on the axes. (`onset` shifts the waveform past the burn-in transient so it plays in the recorded window.) Same hook takes speech, an empirical EEG/LFP trace, or a measured TMS/DBS pulse train. ::: # Wrap-up {background-color="#6880af"} ## Putting it together {.unnumbered} One reproducible workflow, from structure to mechanism: **connectome → local dynamics → coupling → regimes → fitting → stimulation** ::: {.incremental} - A brain network model = structure ($A_{ij},\ \tau_{ij}$) · local dynamics $f$ · coupling $g$ · observation $h$ - **TVB-O** captures all of it as FAIR, machine-readable metadata — reproducible *and* comparable - **Bifurcation analysis** identifies the dynamical regime *before* you fit - **Inference & stimulation** turn the model into testable, mechanistic hypotheses ::: ## Acknowledgements ::: {.r-stretch} ```{=html}
